era_context: Mid-14th century: tensions between England and France are escalating toward the Hundred Years' War; the Black Death is spreading from Central Asia; the Yuan Dynasty in China is weakening; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Avignon Papacy continues; the Mamluk Sultanate controls Egypt and the Levant.

events.title: Anglo-French tensions escalate over Aquitaine and feudal disputes
events.description: Tensions between England and France escalate significantly in 1336 over disputes regarding the Duchy of Aquitaine and feudal obligations. King Edward III of England and King Philip VI of France engage in diplomatic disputes and military posturing. The conflict is rooted in Edward's claim to the French throne (through his mother, Isabella of France) and disputes over English-held territories in France. These tensions are precursors to the formal beginning of the Hundred Years' War in 1337. Both kingdoms are mobilizing military forces and preparing for conflict.

sources.name: English royal records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Anglo-French tensions in 1336 are well-documented in royal records and diplomatic correspondence. The escalation of disputes in this year is firmly established.

events.title: Black Death spreads through Central Asia and toward the Middle East
events.region: Central Asia, Silk Road trade routes
events.description: The Black Death continues its spread westward from Central Asia in 1336. The plague is carried along the Silk Road trade routes by merchants, armies, and travellers. Central Asian cities and trade hubs experience massive mortality. The plague's spread is facilitated by the extensive Mongol Empire's trade networks. By 1336, the plague is approaching the Middle East and North Africa. The disease will eventually reach Europe in 1347, where it will cause devastating mortality. The plague's spread is one of the most significant demographic events of the medieval period.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus from modern epidemiological studies

events.certainty_note: The Black Death's presence in Central Asia in 1336 is supported by modern epidemiological models, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The exact timeline of the plague's spread is debated.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces widespread rebellion and economic crisis
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty (Mongol rule) faces widespread rebellion and economic crisis in 1336. Multiple rebel groups challenge Yuan authority across China. The government's control is weakening, particularly in southern China. Economic problems, including inflation and currency debasement, are destabilizing the state. These uprisings are precursors to the eventual fall of the Yuan Dynasty. The rebellions are driven by economic hardship, resentment of Mongol rule, and the spread of secret societies and religious movements. Zhu Yuanzhang is among the rebel leaders gaining prominence.
events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty instability and rebellions in the 1330s are well-attested, but specific events in 1336 are less precisely dated. The Ming Shilu was compiled retrospectively.

events.title: Ottoman Empire expands in Anatolia under Orhan I
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Orhan I (r. 1323–1362), continues its expansion in Anatolia in 1336. Ottoman forces capture additional territories from neighbouring Beylik states and weakened Byzantine territories. The Ottoman state is becoming increasingly organized and militarized. Orhan is establishing a more formal administrative structure and military system. The Ottomans are transitioning from a small principality to a major regional power. This period marks the beginning of Ottoman expansion that will eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople.
events.certainty_note: Orhan I's expansion in the 1330s is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and Byzantine sources, but specific events in 1336 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate maintains control of Egypt and the Levant
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate, under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341, with interruptions), continues to maintain control of Egypt and the Levant in 1336. The Mamluk state is a major regional power, controlling trade routes and maintaining military dominance. The Sultanate's wealth is derived from control of the spice trade and agricultural production in the Nile Delta. Internal factional conflicts among the Mamluk elite are ongoing, but the state remains stable and powerful.
events.certainty_note: The Mamluk Sultanate's control of Egypt and the Levant in 1336 is well-established. Al-Nasir Muhammad's reign is documented in multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The escalation of Anglo-French tensions in 1336 is sometimes presented as inevitable, but some historians argue that diplomatic solutions were still possible at this point. The conflict was not predetermined; it resulted from specific decisions and miscalculations by both Edward III and Philip VI. The Black Death's origin and early spread are debated; the exact timeline of its westward movement in 1336 is uncertain. Yuan Dynasty rebellions are sometimes conflated across multiple years in later sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1336 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Western European sources. English and French royal records are abundant and contemporary. Sources from the Ottoman Empire, Mamluk Sultanate, and China are either later compilations or fragmentary. The Black Death's early spread is reconstructed from epidemiological models and later chronicles rather than contemporary records. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no contemporary written records for this year. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and court-sponsored chroniclers.

graph_edges.note: Escalating Anglo-French tensions in 1336 led to the formal beginning of the Hundred Years' War in 1337
The Black Death's spread from Central Asia in 1336 led to its arrival in Europe in 1347
Yuan Dynasty rebellions in 1336 contributed to the eventual fall of the Yuan Dynasty in 1368
Ottoman expansion in 1336 was part of the long process that led to the fall of Constantinople in 1453
